full_response:
We must compute the length of the circle defined by “all points at fixed radial coordinate” in each metric.  

(a)  In the metric  
  ds² = du² + cosh²(u)\,dv²,  
we take a circle at u = u₀ = const, with v running from 0 to 2π.  Along this circle du = 0, so  
  ds = √[g_{vv}] dv = cosh(u₀) dv.  
Hence the circumference is  
  C₁ = ∫₀^{2π} cosh(u₀) dv = 2π cosh(u₀).  

(b)  In flat polar coordinates  
  ds² = dr² + r² dφ²,  
a circle at r = r₀ has dr = 0, so  
  ds = r₀ dφ,  
and  
  C₂ = ∫₀^{2π} r₀ dφ = 2π r₀.  

Boxing the results:  
\boxed{  
\begin{aligned}  
\text{(a) } & C_1 = 2\pi\,\cosh(u_0),\\  
\text{(b) } & C_2 = 2\pi\,r_0.  
\end{aligned}  
}  
  
Confidence: 95%
